1.matchText: Zhou Chinese were already dealing with their own nomadic raiders
1.blurb: The Zhou dynasty's feudal system was cracking under pressure from nomadic raiders on the northern frontier — the same steppe peoples whose western cousins were the Scythians. The Zhou built walls and frontier garrisons, but the raids helped trigger the collapse of the Western Zhou in 771 BCE. The steppe conveyor belt connected the Scythians and the Zhou's nomadic enemies across 5,000 miles of grassland.

1.matchText: Olmec civilization at San Lorenzo was at its peak
1.blurb: Around 900 BCE, while the Scythians were pushing into the Pontic steppe, the Olmec were at their height at San Lorenzo in the Gulf Coast lowlands of Mexico — carving colossal stone heads, developing the Mesoamerican ballgame, and building the first monumental architecture in the Americas. Two civilizations at their peak on opposite sides of the world, with no knowledge of each other's existence.

While the Scythians were claiming the Pontic steppe, the Kingdom of Kush was establishing itself at Napata in Upper Nubia — an African kingdom that would soon conquer Egypt itself, producing the 25th Dynasty of pharaohs. Both civilizations were rising on the periphery of the 'civilized' world, and both would shock the settled empires they bordered.
As the Scythians entered the Pontic steppe, the Assyrian Empire was at its terrifying height in Mesopotamia — the settled superpower whose lands the Scythians would soon be raiding.

At the far end of the steppe world, Western Zhou China was sliding toward collapse and its own nomad problem. The grasslands connected China and the Black Sea long before anyone could see the whole chain.
While the Scythians built a horse kingdom, Greece was climbing out of its Dark Age toward the polis — the civilization that would later write the Scythians into history as the archetypal barbarian.
In the Andes, Chavín de Huántar was beginning its rise — a religious civilization forming on the opposite side of the planet, in total mutual ignorance of the steppe.
In Mesoamerica the Olmec capital of San Lorenzo was at its peak — colossal heads and rubber-ball ritual, the 'mother culture,' contemporary with the first Scythians and just as unaware of them.

2.matchText: the world's first superpower, ruling from the Tigris
2.blurb: The Assyrian Empire under Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal was the largest and most militarily sophisticated state the ancient world had yet produced — a machine of organized violence that had conquered Egypt, crushed Babylon, and terrified every kingdom in the Near East. Yet even Assyria found the Scythians useful enough to seek alliance with, marrying a princess to the Scythian king Bartatua to secure the northern frontier.

2.matchText: Croesus would later be conquered by Cyrus the Great
2.blurb: Croesus of Lydia, the king whose wealth became proverbial, was conquered by Cyrus the Great around 547 BCE — barely a century after the Cimmerians (driven out by the Scythians) had nearly destroyed his predecessor's kingdom. The Scythian displacement of the Cimmerians set off a chain reaction across Anatolia that shaped the political landscape Cyrus would eventually conquer.

2.matchText: combined Median and Babylonian army sacked the Assyrian capital
2.blurb: The fall of Nineveh in 612 BCE was the end of the world's first superpower — the Medes and Babylonians finally overwhelmed an empire that had dominated the Near East for three centuries. The Scythians had played kingmaker in the region for a generation, and their withdrawal freed the Medes to deliver the final blow. Without the Scythians propping up Assyria, the empire might have fallen decades earlier.

Ashurbanipal's sack of the Elamite capital Susa in 646 BCE — one of the most thorough acts of destruction in the ancient world — happened during the very decades when the Scythians were dominating the Near East. Scythian warriors and Elamite refugees may have been in the same region at the same time, caught up in the same political convulsions that were reshaping the ancient world.

By the 620s BCE, Egypt under Psammetichus I had just reasserted its independence after decades of Assyrian domination — part of the same Late Period renaissance that would produce the last great flowering of pharaonic culture. The Scythians reaching Egypt's borders was one more shock in a century of upheaval for a civilization trying to rebuild itself after foreign rule.
The Scythian Near Eastern raids hit the Assyrian Empire in its final, overextended century — the Scythians were one of the pressures helping bring the superpower down by 612 BCE.

3.blurb: While the Scythians consolidated their grass kingdom, Cyrus was assembling the Persian Empire — the superpower whose king Darius would soon march into the steppe and be humiliated.

The sixth century BCE is the Axial Age: as the Scythians traded with Greek colonies, Confucius was teaching in China and the Buddha preaching in India. The steppe produced no philosophers — it produced horses, gold, and a way of life that outlasted most Axial empires.

4.matchText: ruler of the largest empire the world had ever seen
4.blurb: Darius I built the most sophisticated administrative state the ancient world had produced — 23 satrapies connected by royal roads, relay riders, standardized coinage, and a multilingual bureaucracy. He carved his achievements into the cliff at Behistun for the world to see. None of it mattered on the steppe. The Scythians humiliated the King of Kings by refusing to fight and letting the empty grassland do the work.
Darius's Scythian fiasco (513 BCE) falls right between the founding of the Roman Republic (509) and Marathon (490) — the Persian superpower made to look foolish by a people with no state at all.
A generation after Darius failed in the steppe, the same Persian war machine would fail again at Marathon — the Scythians had shown the empire's limits first.

5.blurb: The Scythian golden age overlaps the Athenian classical age — Pericles, the Parthenon, Plato. Greek writers idealized the Scythians as 'the most just of peoples,' a fantasy that says more about Greek self-doubt than about the steppe.
At the same moment, China was tearing itself apart in the Warring States — while the Scythians, with no state to fracture, simply rode on.
India was a generation away from its first great empire under the Mauryas — the settled world building ever-larger states as the Scythians kept theirs stateless by design.

6.matchText: the founder of the Achaemenid Empire, the conqueror of Babylon
6.blurb: Cyrus the Great conquered the Medes, Lydians, and Babylonians to build the largest empire the world had seen — a testament to military genius and political pragmatism. But on the northeastern frontier, facing the Massagetae under Queen Tomyris, his luck ran out. The greatest empire-builder of his age was killed by steppe nomads, a pattern that would repeat for two millennia.

Fu Hao, a consort of the Shang king Wu Ding, commanded armies of up to 13,000 soldiers around 1200 BCE and was buried with bronze weapons, jade ornaments, and sacrificial victims. Her tomb at Yinxu is one of the richest Shang burials ever found. Like the Scythian warrior women, Fu Hao challenges the assumption that ancient warfare was exclusively male — though her case was exceptional where Scythian women warriors were a recognized social category.

Scythian warrior-women have scattered parallels elsewhere — the Shang queen Fu Hao led armies around 1200 BCE — but nowhere else was female participation in war a broad cultural norm the way the steppe seems to have made it.

7.blurb: Chinese silk found in the Pazyryk tombs of the Altai Mountains is some of the earliest physical evidence of Silk Road trade — centuries before the formal overland route was established under the Han dynasty. The silk traveled 3,000 miles from Chinese workshops to a Siberian grave, passing through the hands of intermediaries whose identities we can only guess at. The steppe was already functioning as Eurasia's commercial highway.

7.matchText: Vedic Hindus memorized their hymns for over a millennium
7.blurb: The Vedic tradition preserved the Rigveda — over a thousand hymns totaling 10,000 verses — through purely oral transmission for over a millennium before anyone wrote them down. The memorization techniques were so precise that the text survived essentially unchanged. The Scythians made a similar choice to keep their knowledge oral, but unlike the Vedic Hindus, they left no literary descendants to eventually record what they knew.

The Pazyryk tombs are contemporary with the height of Achaemenid Persia and the Athenian golden age — and the Chinese silk inside them proves the steppe was already linking the two ends of Eurasia centuries before the 'Silk Road' had a name.
Chinese silk in a Siberian tomb: while Warring States China fought itself, its luxury goods were already moving west along steppe networks the Scythians had run for centuries.

8.matchText: who was busy conquering the Persian Empire
8.blurb: While Zopyrion was getting his army annihilated in Scythian territory, Alexander the Great was dismantling the Persian Empire — defeating Darius III at Gaugamela and burning Persepolis. Alexander's conquest of Persia reshaped the entire ancient world, but his general's failed Scythian invasion proved that the steppe remained unconquerable even when every settled empire had been brought to heel.

In 221 BCE, while the Scythians were being compressed by the Sarmatians, Qin Shi Huang was unifying China by force — and immediately turning his attention to the nomadic threat on his northern frontier. The Qin built the first connected sections of the Great Wall to keep out steppe raiders, the Chinese answer to the same problem the Scythians posed to every settled civilization: how do you fight people who refuse to stand still?
